Key Antiseizure Selection Tips

Drugs rarely, if ever, used in pediatrics: carbamazepine (Tegretol), phenytoin (Dilantin).

Drug rash risk

Avoid if prior serious rash such as SJS/TEN, DRESS.

  • Higher‑risk: phenytoin (DPH), carbamazepine, oxcarbazepine, lamotrigine, zonisamide, felbamate.
  • Consider instead: levetiracetam, valproate, benzodiazepines, brivaracetam, lacosamide.

Sulfonamide (“sulfa”) issues

  • Contain sulfa moiety: topiramate, zonisamide, acetazolamide.
  • In true severe sulfonamide allergy, most avoid these and favor levetiracetam, valproate, benzodiazepines, carbamazepine/oxcarbazepine (if no serious rash history), lamotrigine with counseling, etc.

Renal failure

Dose‑adjust / avoid accumulation.

  • Need adjustment or extended interval: gabapentin, levetiracetam, zonisamide, topiramate.
  • More hepatic/less renal clearance (often preferred in advanced CKD, with their own caveats): valproate, carbamazepine, oxcarbazepine, lacosamide, clobazam.

Hepatic dysfunction

  • Prefer: levetiracetam, lacosamide.
  • Avoid or use great caution: valproate, carbamazepine, phenytoin, phenobarbital, chronic benzodiazepines.

Weight/metabolic concerns

  • Better: lamotrigine, levetiracetam; topiramate/zonisamide can cause weight loss but have cognitive/renal stone risks.
  • Less ideal: valproate, carbamazepine, pregabalin.

Kidney stones/metabolic acidosis

  • Avoid or monitor closely: topiramate, zonisamide, acetazolamide.
  • Better: lamotrigine, levetiracetam, valproate.

Psychiatric comorbidity

  • Mood‑friendly: lamotrigine, valproate (bipolar spectrum), clobazam.
  • Potentially mood‑worsening: levetiracetam, brivaracetam, topiramate, phenobarbital.

Women of childbearing potential

  • Prefer: lamotrigine, levetiracetam.
  • Avoid if possible: valproate (especially generalized epilepsies), topiramate, phenytoin, phenobarbital.

Generalized epilepsies

  • Use: valproate (if feasible), levetiracetam, lamotrigine, ethosuximide (absence), clobazam, topiramate/zonisamide.
  • Avoid: carbamazepine, oxcarbazepine, phenytoin, gabapentin, tiagabine.

Focal epilepsies

  • Use: carbamazepine, oxcarbazepine, lamotrigine, levetiracetam, lacosamide, cenobamate (older patients).
  • Avoid: ethosuximide (absence‑only), vigabatrin unless specific indication.

Cognitive concerns

  • Better: lamotrigine, levetiracetam, brivaracetam.
  • Worse: topiramate, phenobarbital, chronic benzodiazepines, high‑dose zonisamide.

Polypharmacy / interaction‑heavy patients

  • Preferred: levetiracetam, lacosamide, brivaracetam, gabapentin/pregabalin.
  • Caution: carbamazepine, phenytoin, phenobarbital, primidone (inducers), valproate (inhibitor).

“Special” non‑standard / adjuncts

  • Anakinra: consider in FIRES/NORSE or cytokine‑mediated epileptic encephalopathy when standard ASMs and first‑line immunotherapy fail.
  • Steroids/immunotherapy: for suspected autoimmune epilepsy, NORSE/FIRES, or clear inflammatory evidence.
  • Cenobamate: very effective for refractory focal‑onset seizures in adults/older teens; slow titration due to DRESS and cardiac concerns.

Status Epilepticus Pathway

A definition and classification of status epilepticus – Report of the ILAE Task Force on Classification of Status Epilepticus” 2015:

Status Epilepticus – Key Time Points (ILAE 2015)

Concept: A seizure that lasts too long, or a series of seizures without recovery, because normal "stop" mechanisms fail.

t1 = when you treat as status. t2 = when brain injury becomes a concern.

Operational Times by Seizure Type

Type of SEt1 – treat as statust2 – risk of long‑term problems
Convulsive (generalized tonic–clonic)5 minutes30 minutes

Neonatal Seizure Diagnosis and Treatment – Quick Pathway

(Term / late‑preterm infants, <28 days CGA)

1. Suspected Seizure

StepKey actions
Initial assessmentAttempt to record video if safe; note semiology and duration; document vital signs; check glucose and electrolytes per local protocol.
Emergent treatment if seizure highly suspectedGive phenobarbital (PHB) 20 mg/kg IV load (preferred) or lorazepam 0.1 mg/kg IV if PHB not immediately available. Discuss with neurology and initiate continuous EEG (cEEG) urgently.

2. Confirmation

SituationManagement
Seizure confirmed on cEEG or suspicion remains very highTreat with PHB 20 mg/kg IV as first‑line antiseizure medication (counting any emergent dose toward this total). Aim for PHB level in at least the low‑ to mid‑20 µg/mL range.

3. Second‑Line Therapy

StepOptions / notes
Seizures persist after first PHB load1) Give an additional 10–20 mg/kg PHB (dose guided by blood pressure and respiratory status), and/or 2) Add a second‑line agent: levetiracetam 60 mg/kg IV or fosphenytoin 20 mg/kg IV (as PE). Levetiracetam is often favored in neonates with cardiac disease; fosphenytoin may be preferred when a sodium‑channel–responsive channelopathy is suspected.

4. Third‑Line and Refractory Therapy

LevelTreatment
Third lineUse whichever of levetiracetam 60 mg/kg, fosphenytoin 20 mg/kg, or lacosamide 4–8 mg/kg has not yet been given, based on local protocol and evolving safety data.
Fourth lineMidazolam: 0.2 mg/kg IV bolus, then continuous infusion at 0.2 mg/kg/h. Check EEG about every 15 minutes; if seizures continue, repeat 0.2 mg/kg bolus and increase infusion by 0.1–0.2 mg/kg/h to a maximum of 2 mg/kg/h.
If still refractory or midazolam not toleratedConsider ketamine infusion or other ICU‑level therapies after discussion with neurology/intensive care. In select refractory cases, PHB levels up to ~80–100 µg/mL may be used with close hemodynamic and airway support.

5. Ongoing Monitoring and Weaning

ItemRecommendation
cEEG durationContinue cEEG until the infant has been seizure‑free on EEG for at least 24 hours, unless there is a confirmed neonatal epilepsy syndrome or care is being redirected.
Skin‑integrity precautionsPremature infants, those on ECMO, pharmacologically paralyzed infants, and those in incubators are at high risk for skin breakdown with prolonged electrodes; remove or reposition leads as soon as clinically safe.
Maintenance ASMAfter seizures are controlled, consider PHB 5 mg/kg/day (often divided BID or given QHS) if large PHB loads were used. If levetiracetam was loaded or ongoing risk is high, use maintenance LEV 40–60 mg/kg/day divided every 8–12 hours. Because most neonatal seizures are acute symptomatic, if the acute cause has resolved and there is no concern for neonatal epilepsy, aim to wean ASM before discharge or within a short, planned outpatient interval.

Acute Symptomatic Neonatal Seizures

Definition: Acute symptomatic (acute provoked) neonatal seizures are electrographic seizures that occur in close temporal association with an acute brain insult or systemic disturbance such as hypoxic‑ischemic encephalopathy, stroke, intracranial hemorrhage, infection, or significant metabolic derangement, in an infant without prior epilepsy.

In current neonatal ILAE frameworks, seizures are defined electrographically: sudden, repetitive, evolving, and stereotyped EEG discharges with clear onset, evolution, and offset; clinical signs may be absent or subtle.

Levels of Diagnostic Certainty

LevelDefinitionUsual action
1 – Definite seizureClinical event with a continuous EEG seizure correlate.Treat as seizure.
2 – Probable seizureClinically clear focal clonic or focal tonic seizure, or seizure confirmed on amplitude‑integrated EEG.Treat as seizure.
3 – Possible seizureEvent suspicious but not classic, or EEG correlation unclear.Consider treatment based on overall context; discuss with neurology.
4 – Suspected seizureInsufficient evidence to meet seizure criteria.Generally do not treat as seizure alone.
5 – Not a seizureMovement shown by EEG or exam to be non‑epileptic.Do not treat as seizure.

Natural Course of Seizure Burden After Acute Neonatal Brain Injury

(Especially hypoxic‑ischemic encephalopathy with continuous EEG monitoring.)

FeatureTypical pattern
Time to first seizureMany infants have their first electrographic seizure within the first 12–24 hours of life, often during the early phase of therapeutic hypothermia.
Time of peak seizure burdenSeizure burden generally peaks between about 12 and 24 hours after birth or after injury, with a median around the early 20‑hour range in published cohorts.
Duration of seizure periodIn many infants, seizures continue for roughly 2–3 days after onset, with most seizure activity occurring within the first 72 hours. Time from first to peak seizure burden is typically shorter than from peak to last seizure.
Clinical implicationExpect seizure burden to ramp up over the first day, remain significant for another 24–48 hours, and usually decline by around 72 hours if the injury is not rapidly worsening. Because higher total seizure burden is associated with worse neurodevelopmental outcomes, aggressive monitoring and treatment over this early 72‑hour window are crucial.

IV Pyridoxine (vitamin B6) challenge

  • Use 100mg pyridoxine IV to be pushed over 1-3 minutes. It can also be slowly infused in over 15-30 min as IV push may cause burning
  • If no clinical response, the dose should be repeated up to a maximum of 500 mg, although corresponding change on EEG may be delayed by several hours
  • Have patient on CR monitor, cEEG, and vitals q 5 minutes
  • Provider should be at the bedside during the injection/push and 5-10 min after injection to watch for any SEs (risk of apnea, cardiorespiratory depression, profound sedation) and to look at EEG monitoring.
  • Can alternatively begin supplementation with 30 mg/kg/day of pyridoxine while awaiting gene testing (less risk of hemodynamic instability than the IV challenge)
  • Should have seizure resolution within 3-7 days once pyridoxine supplementation has started

Ketogenic Diet (KD)

High-fat (80–90%), adequate protein, limited carbohydrate diet used as adjunctive therapy for drug-resistant epilepsy.

Indications and Efficacy

StudyKey finding
Neal 2008 (randomized prospective)38% had >50% seizure reduction; 7% seizure-free (vs 6% controls, 0% seizure-free).
Johns Hopkins 19977% seizure-free; 20% with >90% reduction; 50% with >50% reduction after 1 year.
Freitas 2007 (54 children)57% achieved >75% seizure control.
Cochrane 2016Seizure freedom up to 55% and seizure reduction up to 85% in the 4:1 KD group after 3 months.

Key indications:

  • GLUT-1 transporter deficiency (first-line treatment)
  • Tonic, atonic, and atypical absence seizures
  • Infantile spasms
  • Dravet syndrome
  • Doose syndrome / Epilepsy with Myoclonic-Atonic Seizures (EMAS)
  • FIRES (Febrile Infection-Related Epilepsy Syndrome)

Epileptologist consultation is needed prior to referring a patient for the ketogenic diet.

Pre-Diet Evaluation

Pre-ketogenic labs:

  • CMP, CBC, lactate/pyruvate, urine organic acids, serum amino acids
  • Acylcarnitine profile, phosphorus, fasting lipid panel
  • Carnitine, 25-OH vitamin D, Mg, Zn, selenium

Monitoring, Supplementation, and Adverse Effects

Required supplements:

  • Multivitamin, calcium, and vitamin D supplementation
  • Monitor for deficiencies in: carnitine, zinc, selenium, vitamin D, vitamin K, phosphorus, fiber, bicarbonate

Adverse effects:

CommonLess common / serious
Kidney stones, constipation, increased lipids, decreased growth, acidosis, weight loss, GERDOsteopenia, anemia, prolonged QT, cardiomyopathy, pancreatitis, hypoproteinemia, leukopenia

Initiation (EMU or Inpatient)

  • Monday admission (9 AM) to EMU (hospitalization per keto team and epileptologist discretion).
  • Patients do not need to fast for this admission.
  • Non-caloric beverages only on admission; formula options: RCF, Ketocal 3:1 or 4:1.
  • Day one fasting labs: BMP and lipids; liquid diet for first day for 100% of calories.

Illness, NPO, and On-Call Management

Basic principles: Maintain ketosis. Avoid carbs. Average bicarb 18–23, BS 50–80 (if on TPX/ZGN may have lower bicarb).

ScenarioRecommended action
High-carb food ingestedContinue current ratio; do not fast. Try adding extra fat (tablespoon of butter or oil for PO-fed; continue keto formula for G-tube).
Vomiting / refusing to eatGive ½ meal quantity and double the number of meals per day. If still refusing, try liquid ketogenic replacement (sip all day). If still refusing, carb replacement: 1½–2 oz OJ or apple juice up to every 4 hours. Pedialyte is OK (less carb, more volume needed). Encourage 0-calorie/caffeine fluids.
Ketones low / increased seizuresEvaluate as if not on diet (missed meds, illness, ASM changes). Keto-specific: Have ketones dropped? Any ratio/calorie changes? High-carb food or new meds? AM ketones are typically lower than PM. Intervention: Remove hidden carb food or change med form; revert to prior calorie/ratio; use IN midazolam or rectal Diastat if needed.
DehydrationIncrease fluid intake to 1.5× maintenance. If unable to tolerate PO, IV hydration with non-dextrose fluids (NS for bolus). If concerned, seek ED with Paramedic/ED letter. If back/abd pain ± dark/bloody urine → suspect kidney stone → increase fluids to 130%+ maintenance, try Cytra-K. If severe pain/emesis ± fever → consider pancreatitis.
HypoglycemiaIf BS >40: no dextrose, NS only. If BS <40: give 4–5 g dextrose, recheck with goal 40–80. D5W = 5 g dextrose per 100 mL; D10W = 10 g per 100 mL. Do not give >10% dextrose through peripheral IV. Ketones will return to pre-illness levels after recovery.
NPO and procedural planningKeto patients should not be NPO >12–13 hours without glucose monitoring (q1h while NPO). Metabolic patients: max 8 hours fasting, consult genetics/metabolics. Give meal or full replacement liquid meal just before NPO. Carb-free clear liquids until 2 hours before procedure. No carbs for 8 hours before sedation (water only). In metabolic disorders, defer fluid management to metabolic team.
Admission for other problemsAny keto child admitted >24 hours → transfer to main campus for keto support. Convert NPO/clear liquids to keto-acceptable fluids/meals (juice/soda/Pedialyte → keto eggnog or liquid replacement meal → full solid meals). Child should eat all meals to maintain ketosis and prevent hypoglycemia. Check BG at least once; check urinary ketones and consider BHB. If admitted overnight and maintaining PO/GT → IVF without dextrose. If NPO: IV without carbs, give dextrose (juice) if BS <40; or give continuous D5W diluted with saline, start max 1–2 g/hr until BS >40, titrate to keep glucose 40–80.

Medication Considerations

  • Acceptable forms: Tablets, capsules, IM, IV, rectal, nasal drops, sprays, nebulizers.
  • Avoid: Chewables, oral suspensions/liquids/elixirs (use only if no substitute).
  • Acceptable fever/pain meds: Brand name Tylenol 325 mg tabs/caps, 500 mg caps; Feverall rectal suppositories (80, 120, 325 mg); Children’s Junior Motrin 100 mg caps; Advil 200 mg caps, 100 mg/5 mL (use sparingly — contains some carbs).
  • Addition of concomitant meds including pain management: oral meds must have minimal CHO.

Vagus Nerve Stimulator (VNS)

VNS is not a cure. It is used to help control or lessen the severity of seizures. VNS is discussed prior to implantation at the epilepsy surgery conference to determine eligibility.

Basics for Families

  • The generator is implanted under the skin of the chest; electrodes wrap around the left vagus nerve in the neck.
  • A stimulus is sent to the vagus nerve at programmed intervals to attempt to prevent seizures.
  • Newer devices (Model 106 and Sentiva) monitor heart rate and deliver a slightly higher current for rapid heart-rate changes that may be associated with seizures.
  • VNS is activated just after implantation and ramped up via clinic visits and automatic protocols every 1–2 weeks.
  • Battery lasts 6–10 years; generator must be surgically replaced. The lead on the vagus nerve is generally not removed due to risk of injury.

Using the Magnet

  • Swipe the magnet over the generator for a count of 3 seconds.
  • Once the magnet is removed, the generator provides a higher-amplitude pulse for ~60 seconds to try to abort the seizure or shorten the postictal period.
  • The magnet can never be used too much or damage the device. The VNS can only be activated every 60 seconds regardless of how many times you swipe.

Side Effects and Temporary Shut-Off

  • Side effects: Local discomfort, throat pain, cough, changes in voice pitch/volume, reflux. These can usually be modulated by adjusting VNS settings.
  • OSA can be an adverse effect — increased daytime sleepiness or worsening snoring should be evaluated with PSG.
  • To temporarily turn off: Place the magnet over the generator and tape it in place with heavy tape. As long as the magnet is over the generator, no current is delivered. Remove the magnet to resume normal stimulation.

Imaging and Surgery Considerations

  • The VNS lead is ferromagnetic and may heat up in a strong magnetic field. MRI should not be performed of the chest and upper spine.
  • Before MRI: set output current to zero (can be done by EEG techs/APPs in EMU for inpatients or clinic RNs for outpatients). Restore settings after scan.
  • Electrocautery during surgery can affect the device. Interrogate before and after surgery to ensure proper function. The OR typically requests the device be turned off before surgery and back on after.

Infantile Spasms (Infantile Epileptic Spasms Syndrome)

Overview

Infantile spasms (IS) is an epilepsy of early infancy, affecting 3-4.5/10,000 live births, with typical onset 1-15 months (peak 4-9 months), M>F. Often associated with developmental issues.

Patients can have flexor, extensor, mixed flexor-extensor, and/or asymmetric spasms. IS can mimic reflux or myoclonus. Patients are often admitted for continuous EEG to definitively evaluate for infantile spasms.

Prognosis

  • Resolution of spasms is associated with better developmental outcomes. The primary driving factor is treatment with a standard first-line therapy (ACTH, prednisolone, or vigabatrin).
  • In children with known etiology, prognosis is related to etiology. Children without prior developmental delay or prior seizures may have a better prognosis.
  • 50–60% develop other seizure types in the future; about 35% develop Lennox–Gastaut syndrome.

<3 spike foci but >1 channel with abnormal high-amplitude

4

(Probable EE)

>3 spike foci <50% of one second bins AND >1 channel with abnormal high amplitude, OR

Not meeting criteria for 5 but includes GMFS or paroxysmal voltage attenuations

5

(Definite EE)

>3 spike foci that are >50% of one second bins

TherapyProsCons / risks

Prednisolone (high‑dose oral steroid)Least expensive; oral administration; similar effectiveness to high‑dose ACTH in several modern series when dosed adequately.Same steroid adverse effects as ACTH (immune suppression, edema, GI bleeding, weight gain, irritability, sleep disturbance, hypertension, cortical atrophy, HPA suppression, rare death); may be slightly less effective than ACTH in some cohorts.

Follow-up for all therapies

Following the initiation of any first-line treatment, efficacy of therapy should be assessed by 2 weeks. Short-term outcomes include cessation of spasms and EEG confirmation of resolution of hypsarrhythmia.

  • EEG should be used to assess for resolution of spasms and hypsarrhythmia at 12-16 days.
  • Clinic visit with pediatric neurology should occur at the same time frame as repeat EEG.
  • If spasms or hypsarrhythmia persist at 2 weeks, consider switching treatment to an alternative first-line treatment with a different mechanism of action. Follow the weaning protocols outlined for the specific first-line agent. Efficacy of the alternative therapy should be assessed at 2 weeks.
  • Consider repeat EEG 1-4 weeks after completion of treatment to confirm sustained efficacy. Also consider repeat EEG if there is concern for developmental plateau/regression or clinical seizures.
  • PCP and neurology should monitor neurodevelopment closely and involve Early Intervention services when appropriate. A diagnosis of IS qualifies for Early Intervention.

Seizure Basics and Classification

Core Concepts

Seizures Seizures result from abnormal excessive rhythmic neurologic electrical activity. This abnormal activity can occur in both hemispheres (generalized seizure) or within one hemisphere (focal seizure), which may spread to the entire brain. Generalized tonic-clonic seizures – motions of which consist of a tonic phase with muscle stiffening, followed by a clonic phase with rhythmic muscle contractions. Focal seizures more often occur in the setting of cerebral insult. Unlike generalized seizures, symptoms of focal seizures vary according to the anatomic location of the abnormal electrical activity. Focal seizures can generalize secondarily, known as focal to bilateral tonic clinic seizures (FTBCS; assuming have a tonic-clonic motoric phase).

Seizures can be motor or non-motor. Non-motor seniologies include behavioral arrest, staring, autonomic signs (pupillary dilation, flushing, retching/rising sensation, emotional symptoms).

Seizures can subclassified as having impaired awareness or without impaired awareness.

Seizures can be classified as provoked or unprovoked. Provoked seizures are those with identifiable causes, which can be isolated to the brain, or are thought to occur secondary to a systemic disorder or illness. Such causes include: brain trauma, CNS infection (i.e. meningitis, encephalitis, brain abscess), anoxic brain injury, intracranial hemorrhage or surgery, metabolic disorders, illicit drug abuse or intoxication (most commonly tricyclic antidepressants and isoniazid), or alcohol withdrawal. Seizures may also occur in the setting of metabolic derangements (hypoglycemia or hyponatremia).

 Unprovoked seizures are those with no discernible cause, or those occurring greater than seven days following precipitating factors or events.

Epilepsy diagnosis:

  • At least two unprovoked seizures occurring more than 24 hours apart.
  • One unprovoked seizure and a probability of further seizures that is similar to the general recurrence risk after two unprovoked seizures (ie ≥ 60 %)
  • Diagnosis of an epilepsy syndrome.
  • Considered resolved: If seizure-free for 10 years with no seizure medications for 5 years.

First Unprovoked Afebrile Seizure

Practice Parameter: evaluating a First Nonfebrile Seizure in Children. Neurology 2000;55:616-62

Diagnostics to rule out symptomatic etiologies in the ED:

  • Electrolytes if the child is < 6 months old; otherwise not needed if the seizure has stopped and the child looks well, and has no known reason for electrolyte derangement.
  • UTOX if prolonged post-ictal state or any suspicion (have a low threshold, strongly consider in adolescents)
  • CBC and UA if infection suspected in an infant or child.
  • LP: Strongly consider in all infants <6 months

Neuroimaging:

  • Brain imaging is often not needed for generalized seizures or recognizable self-limited epilepsy syndromes
  • Imaging is indicated for:
    • Patients of any age with focal findings during or after the seizure
    • Focal EEG abnormality (unless the features are consistent with a known epilepsy syndrome)
    • Abnormal patient exam such as motor or limb size asymmetry or skin findings associated with brain
    • Abnormalities such as neurofibromatosis, tuberous sclerosis, and patches of discolored hair (which can be associated with underlying focal cortical dysplasia)
    • Any concern for child abuse or traumatic cause of seizures – in this case, consider CT for evaluation

EEG:

  • Outpatient EEG in all patients within 2 weeks.
  • If not returning to baseline—consider obtaining to rule out nonconvulsive status

Management:

  • Observe in ED for 6 hours after seizure, longer in infants/toddlers (consider admission)
  • No ASMs
  • Neurology appt in new onset seizure clinic or any provider
  • Seizure precautions
  • Rescue: Diastat/Intranasal Versed

Recurrence risk

  • In a long-term, prospective cohort study in the Bronx, otherwise healthy children with normal interictal EEGs after a single unprovoked seizure had seizure recurrence rates of 28% and 32% at 2 and 5 years, while children with abnormal EEGs had seizure recurrence rates of 52% and 59% at 2 and 5 years.

SUDEP – Sudden Unexpected Death in Epilepsy

Definition: Sudden, unexpected, non‑traumatic, non‑drowning death in a person with epilepsy, with or without a witnessed seizure, and no structural or toxicologic cause identified at autopsy.

How Common Is SUDEP?

(Approximate, order‑of‑magnitude figures for counseling.)

PopulationApproximate incidence
Children with epilepsy≈0.2–0.5 per 1000 patient‑years (about 1 per 2,000–5,000 children per year).
Adults with epilepsy≈1–2 per 1000 patient‑years (about 1 per 1,000 per year).
Drug‑resistant / surgery‑candidate cohortsUp to ~9 per 1000 patient‑years in some series.
Dravet syndrome / SCN1A‑related epilepsyCumulative SUDEP risk substantially higher; older cohorts estimate several to ~10–20% across childhood and young adulthood.

Major Risk Factors

(Focus on modifiable items)

Risk factorPractical takeaway
Frequent generalized tonic–clonic seizures (especially ≥3/year)Strongest known risk factor; reducing GTCS frequency is the main protective strategy.
Nocturnal GTCS and sleeping aloneMany SUDEPs occur at night; consider room‑sharing, reliable monitors, or seizure‑alert devices in high‑risk patients.
Poor adherence or abrupt ASM withdrawalReview adherence at each visit; warn against suddenly stopping medications.
Long duration of active epilepsy, drug resistance, polytherapyMarkers of severe disease; refer to epilepsy center for surgery, ketogenic diet, or device options when appropriate.
Developmental disability, young adult ageNon‑modifiable background risks; emphasize supervision, clear rescue plans, and support during transitions to independence.

Counseling Script (At‑a‑Glance)

“There is a small but real risk of sudden death related to epilepsy called SUDEP. In children, it may be as high as about 1 in 2–3 thousand each year in some groups.”

“Risk is highest in people who have frequent big convulsive seizures, especially at night. The main goal is to reduce or eliminate those seizures.”

“Taking medicine every day, avoiding missed doses, treating clusters quickly, and having some form of nighttime supervision or alarm all help lower risk.”

“This topic should be revisited over time, especially as adolescents gain independence.”

Febrile Seizures

Definition: Seizure accompanied by fever (temperature ≥ 100.4°F or 38°C by any method withing surrounding 24hr period), without intracranial / central nervous system infection, that occurs in infants and children 6 - 60 months of age.

Epidemiology: 2-5% of children, 90% within first 3 years

Simple febrile seizure: Brief <15 minutes and generalized

Complex febrile seizure: Duration >15 minutes OR more than 1 seizure in 24 hours OR focal features; 20-30% of cases

NB: with re: multiple within 24 hours (evidence suggests outcomes are similar to simple febrile sz - 'simple febrile sz flurry')

Evaluation

  • Important to consider that 2-5% of children with meningitis present as an apparent febrile sz
  • AAP recommends strongly considering LP for unvaccinated children 6-12 months of age
  • Strongly consider LP in children with complex febrile seizures < 12 months
  • Head CT only when concern for trauma or increased ICP
  • EEG is not helpful in simple febrile seizure

If complex, may consider EEG --> if focal findings, will likely have brain MRI  

- Ideally, EEG when back to baseline (not in post-ictal state)

Treatments:

Simple febrile seizure:

  • Geared towards underlying illness, (common with Roseola, Shigella, Varicella)

Aggressive antipyretic therapy has not been shown to decrease risk of febrile seizures, as seizure is related to rate of temperature change, and frequently occurs before fever is documented

Some old efficacy data for ppx ASMs, but risks generally > benefits (Ped. ’99; 103:1307).

For complex febrile seizures marked by clusters, consider rescue medication

  • Does not generally need EEG or imaging
  • Still consider LP if c/f meningitis (NB: meningeal signs are difficult to observe in infants/toddlers)
  • Generally, do not require rescue med or neurology follow-up -- though can be tailored to situation

Complex febrile seizure:

  • Consider rescue med if recurrent seizures, family lives far away, etc; defer to attending
  • Consider OP EEG, MRI, and neurology follow-up if seizure was focal
  • Consider LP in children with complex febrile seizures < 12 months

PROGNOSIS

Prognosis after Simple:
• Increased risk of recurrent febrile seizures

  • ~30-33% will have one or more recurrences
  • ~10% with 3 or more recurrences
  • Most (75%) recurrent febrile seizures occur within 1 year
  • No evidence of increased mortality, hemiplegia, or mental retardation.
    • Risk of epilepsy shown to be only slightly higher than that of the general population (1 --> 1.7%)

Risk Factors for First Febrile Seizure:

  • Family history of febrile seizures
  • Neonatal discharge ≥ 28 days
  • Delayed development
  • Very high fever
  • Child care attendance
  • Low serum sodium

Risk Factors for Recurrence of Febrile Seizure:

  • Younger age (<18 months)
  • Family history of febrile seizures (1st degree relative)
  • Shorter duration of fever before the initial seizure (<1hr)
  • Relatively lower fever at the time of initial seizure (<39C)
  • Multiple seizures during febrile illness
  • Daycare attendance
  • Family history of epilepsy

Risk Factors for the Development of Epilepsy Following Febrile Seizures:

  • Suspected or abnormal development before the first seizure
  • Family history of afebrile seizures
  • Complex first febrile seizure

What to tell families

There is no evidence that simple febrile seizures cause any structural damage or cognitive decline

Rule of 3s, about 3% of kids have febrile seizures, about 1/3 of those will have recurrent febrile seizures, ½ of those will have a 3rd, and the risk of epilepsy is about 3x greater than general population (but still only 3%)

Key References

  • Patel and Vidaurre. Complex Febrile Seizures: A Practical Guide to Evaluation and Treatment. J Child Neurol, 2013.
  • AAP Clinical Practice Guideline: Long-term Management of Simple Febrile Seizures. Pediatrics 2008;121:1281–1286.
  • Shinnar and Glauser. Febrile Seizures. J Child Neurol 2002;17:S44–S52.

Epilepsy and Driving

  • If child has genetic generalized epilepsy or history of absence seizures (ie JAE, JME), most would obtain 24 hour EMU study to evaluate for subtle clinical seizures
  • If noted to have discharges lasting 3 seconds or longer (or shorter with clinical correlate) most would not recommend driving
  • Consensus: should be 6 months seizure-free without changes to anti seizure medications prior to driving
  • Counseling recommendation:
  • If you miss even a single dose of medication, no driving for 2-3 days
  • If you don’t get a good night’s sleep, no driving for 1-2 days
  • If any concern for seizure, stop driving immediately and call and you must be re-cleared to drive

Australian driving decision tree, for reference: Somerville et al, A decision tree to determine fitness to drive in epilepsy: Results of a pilot in two Australian studies. Epilepsia, 60(7): 2019.

General EEG Interpretation Tips

  • For on‑call questions, when interpretation and/or management is uncertain, personally review and make an individual assessment, then reach out to the on-call epileptologist and/or the primary attending for their input.
  • In complex or chronic epilepsies (e.g., Lennox–Gastaut), compare with prior EEGs when available; technologists can retrieve earlier studies.
  • In young children, arousal patterns (sudden high‑amplitude semi‑rhythmic bursts from sleep) are a common seizure mimic; always review video.

Neonatal EEG – Quick Tips

  • Review neonatal studies at a faster paper speed (e.g., 15 mm/sec) to better appreciate evolution.
  • Recognize common artifacts (patting, sucking); correlate closely with video.
  • Neonatal seizures may be very focal (even a single channel) and low voltage.
  • Electrographic neonatal seizure: rhythmic evolving pattern lasting ≥10 seconds; shorter (<10 s) evolving discharges without clear clinical correlate are often termed brief rhythmic discharges (BRDs).
  • Always check for clinical correlate on video before labeling subtle events as seizures.

Sleep Stages on EEG

StageKey EEG features
Wake (Stage W)Posterior dominant rhythm present; desynchronized background with eye opening.
DrowsinessFluctuating alpha, fronto‑central slowing, overall voltage loss.
N1Vertex waves; positive occipital sharp transients of sleep (POSTS).
N2Sleep spindles and K‑complexes; vertex waves and POSTS may persist.
N3Slow‑wave sleep with prominent delta activity; spindles may persist.
REM (Stage R)Low‑voltage fast activity, no alpha, rapid lateral eye movements.

Background Rhythms

RhythmTypical frequencyUsual contextKey points
Alpha8–13 HzPosterior dominant rhythm in relaxed wakefulness, eyes closed.Disappears with eye opening or mental effort; persistent anterior, non‑reactive alpha suggests alpha coma.
Beta>13 HzSymmetric, frontally predominant.Enhanced by benzodiazepines, barbiturates, and some structural lesions (“barbiturates, benzodiazepines, bad brain”).
Theta4–7 HzNormal in drowsiness and sleep at any age; normal awake in young children.Excess in awake older children/adults suggests diffuse dysfunction.
Delta<4 Hz (3 Hz or less in many texts)Normal in deep sleep in all ages; normal in infants/young children.In awake adults, focal delta suggests local pathology; diffuse delta suggests generalized dysfunction/encephalopathy.

Theta and delta together are referred to as slow waves.

Normal Sleep Rhythms

ComponentFeatures
Vertex waveNegative potential maximal at Cz; prominent in stage 1 and 2 sleep and during arousal.
Sleep spindle11–14 Hz burst lasting 1–2 seconds; maximal at C3/C4; hallmark of N2 sleep.
K‑complexLarge, biphasic wave (negative then slow positive) often followed by a spindle; prominent in N2 sleep and partial arousals.
POSTSPositive occipital transients of sleep; maximal at O1/O2; benign N1–N2 feature.

Key EEG Events and Abnormalities

TermDefinition / description
SpikeSharp transient lasting ~25–70 ms.
Sharp waveSharp transient lasting ~70–200 ms.
Slow waveIndividual waves in theta (4–13 Hz in some texts) or delta (<4 Hz) range.
Sharply contoured slow waveSlow wave with sharply angled morphology, duration >200 ms.
Spike–wave complexSpike followed by a slow wave.
Epileptiform dischargeSpikes, sharp waves, or spike–wave complexes that stand out from background and suggest predisposition to epilepsy.
Posterior dominant rhythmOccipital rhythm of fairly narrow frequency band (alpha); may have superimposed faster or slower activity.

Abnormal EEG Activity

CategoryDifferentiationTypical description / significance
Epileptiform – single vs repetitiveSingle discharge vs frequent, near‑continuous discharges.Single discharges are typically interictal; repetitive discharges ≥1/sec and faster are often associated with clinical seizures.
Epileptiform – focal vs multifocal vs generalizedSpatial distribution.Focal discharges suggest local structural lesion or circuit abnormality; multifocal often developmental or metabolic; generalized typically genetic/developmental generalized epilepsy.
Non‑epileptiform – attenuation / suppressionReduced amplitude, up to near‑flat tracing.Focal attenuation: local structural or extra‑axial process (e.g., fluid collection). Generalized attenuation/suppression: severe diffuse dysfunction.
Non‑epileptiform – slowingFocal vs generalized; polymorphic vs rhythmic.Focal slowing: local structural lesion, often cortical–subcortical. Generalized synchronous slow: metabolic, toxic, or deep lesions; asynchronous diffuse slow: multifocal or diffuse encephalopathy.
Non‑epileptiform – abnormal frequency compositionExcess fast or slow activity in unexpected regions.Example: prominent frontal alpha in a patient with HIE is abnormal, whereas posterior alpha in an awake patient is normal.
Non‑epileptiform – abnormal transientsTransients not strictly epileptiform.Includes patterns such as frontal intermittent rhythmic delta activity (FIRDA) and triphasic waves; usually reflect diffuse or metabolic encephalopathy.

Valproic Acid (VPA) Toxicity

VPA-Induced Hyperammonemic Encephalopathy

Can occur even with therapeutic VPA levels. Suspect in any patient on VPA with acute confusion, lethargy, vomiting, or worsening seizures.

Recognition

Check ammonia level in any encephalopathic patient on valproic acid — even if VPA level is "therapeutic."

  • Ammonia > 80 µmol/L — clinically significant
  • Ammonia > 100 µmol/L — concerning, likely symptomatic
  • Ammonia > 200 µmol/L — critical, risk of cerebral edema

Also check: hepatic function panel (AST, ALT, total/direct bilirubin), VPA level, CBC with platelets, lipase

L-Carnitine Treatment Protocol

Indication: VPA-induced hyperammonemia with encephalopathy, VPA overdose, or hepatotoxicity.

IV Levocarnitine (Carnitor):

  • Loading dose: 100 mg/kg IV (max 6 g) over 30 minutes
  • Maintenance: 50 mg/kg IV (max 3 g) every 8 hours
  • Continue until ammonia normalizes and mental status improves
  • Transition to oral: 50-100 mg/kg/day divided TID (max 3 g/day)

Additional management:

  • Hold VPA immediately
  • Consider lactulose if ammonia critically elevated (> 200 µmol/L)
  • Monitor ammonia every 4-6 hours until trending down
  • If on VPA + other enzyme inducers (phenytoin, carbamazepine, phenobarbital) → higher risk of carnitine depletion

VPA Overdose

  • VPA level > 150 mg/L: Significant toxicity likely — L-carnitine, supportive care
  • VPA level > 300 mg/L: Severe — consider hemodialysis (VPA is protein-bound at therapeutic levels but saturation occurs at high concentrations, making dialysis more effective)
  • VPA level > 850 mg/L: Life-threatening — hemodialysis strongly recommended
  • Activated charcoal if within 1-2 hours of ingestion (VPA is well-absorbed)
  • No specific antidote — L-carnitine is the primary treatment adjunct
